title: Factorial / Permutations / Combinations Calculator
shortDescription: Calculate n! (factorial), P(n,k) permutations, and C(n,k) combinations. Handles large integers via BigInt up to n=5000.
description: Enter n and k. Returns n!, P(n,k) (ordered selections), and C(n,k) (unordered selections). Useful for probability, statistics, and combinatorics homework.
metaDescription: Free factorial, permutations (nPr), and combinations (nCr) calculator. Big-integer support up to n=5000. Probability and combinatorics ready.

error.tooLargeOrNeg: n must be a non-negative integer ≤ 5000.

sections.heading: Factorial
paragraphs: n! (read 'n factorial') is the product of all positive integers from 1 to n. So 5! = 1×2×3×4×5 = 120. By convention, 0! = 1 (the empty product).
Factorials grow extremely fast. 10! = 3.6M, 20! = 2.4×10¹⁸, 100! has 158 digits. Floating-point breaks at 21! (because of double-precision limit); we use BigInt for exact values up to n=5000.

sections.heading: Permutations vs combinations
paragraphs: Permutations P(n,k) = n! / (n−k)!: number of ordered ways to choose k items from n. P(5,2) = 20: pick first place from 5, second from remaining 4 = 5×4 = 20.
Combinations C(n,k) = n! / (k!(n−k)!): number of unordered ways. C(5,2) = 10: same picks but {first, second} = {second, first} so divide by 2!. The famous 'n choose k'.
Use permutations when order matters (race podium, password order). Use combinations when only the chosen set matters (lottery numbers, committee selection). C(n,k) ≤ P(n,k) always; equal when k=1.

sections.heading: Where these come up
paragraphs: Probability: dice, cards, coin flips. P(3 heads in 5 flips) = C(5,3) × (1/2)⁵ = 10/32. Combinations let you count favorable outcomes.
Statistics: binomial distribution uses C(n,k). Sampling without replacement uses combinations.
Computer science: counting subsets, complexity analysis (e.g., k-clique enumeration is C(n,k)), graph algorithms.
Real-world: lottery odds (US Powerball: C(69,5) × 26 ≈ 292M combinations). Restaurant menu combos: 'pick 3 sides from 8' is C(8,3) = 56 ways.

howTo.name: Enter k (optional)
howTo.text: How many to pick (for P and C calculations). Must be 0 ≤ k ≤ n.

howTo.name: Read all three
howTo.text: n!, P(n,k), C(n,k). Outputs truncated for very large numbers.

faq.a: By convention, the 'empty product' is 1 (just like the empty sum is 0). It also makes formulas like C(n,0) = 1 (one way to choose nothing) work consistently.

faq.q: What's the largest factorial this can compute?
faq.a: n=5000 gives a 16,326-digit number. We cap at 5000 to prevent the browser from freezing on huge inputs. For larger, use a CAS.

faq.q: What's the difference between permutations and combinations?
faq.a: Order matters in permutations, doesn't in combinations. {A,B} is the same combination as {B,A} but two different permutations: AB and BA.

faq.q: Are factorials defined for negative numbers?
faq.a: Not in the standard sense. The gamma function Γ(x) extends factorial to all real numbers (and complex), but our calculator handles only non-negative integers.
C(n,k) = n! / (k! × (n-k)!). Read as 'n choose k'. Equivalently: P(n,k) / k! since order doesn't matter.
Exact for all values within range. We use BigInt arithmetic, which has no floating-point error.

faq.q: Why is 70! so much bigger than 60!?
faq.a: Each factorial multiplies by the next integer. 70! is roughly 60! × 61 × 62 × … × 70 ≈ 60! × 1.4 × 10¹⁷. Factorials grow faster than exponential — they're roughly n^n × e^-n × √(2πn) by Stirling's approximation.

faq.q: Does the data leave my browser?
faq.a: No. Calculation runs locally; nothing is sent to a server.
